source.title: Functional rare and low frequency variants in BLK and BANK1 contribute to human lupus

gist_memory.paper_problem_memory: SLE is a heterogeneous autoimmune disease with a strong genetic component, but common GWAS variants explain only a modest fraction of risk. The study investigates whether rare coding variants in known SLE-risk genes contribute to disease, focusing on functional consequences in BLK and BANK1.

section_gists.heading: Functional rare and low frequency variants in BLK and BANK1 contribute to human lupus
section_gists.gist: The paper introduces the problem of missing heritability in SLE, hypothesizing that rare coding variants in lupus-risk genes may have strong effects. It outlines the study's aim to demonstrate functional consequences of rare variants in BLK and BANK1, which are found in a substantial proportion of patients and impair suppression of IRF5 and type-I IFN.
process_signals: Hypothesis: rare variants with strong effects contribute to SLE, contrasting with common variant additive model.
Focus on BLK and BANK1 due to prior GWAS associations and known interaction.

section_gists.heading: Results
section_gists.gist: Rare coding variants in 76 SLE-risk genes were identified in 82% of SLE patients and 72% of controls. BLK and BANK1 were prioritized for functional studies. Rare BLK variants were found in ~10% of SLE patients, significantly less in controls. Functional assays showed patient-specific BLK variants impaired suppression of IRF5 and type-I IFN in B cells, and BANK1 W40C variant altered localization and TRAF6 interaction. Mouse model with Blk R125W showed increased pathogenic T cells on lupus-prone background.
process_signals: Initial screening of 69 SLE probands (SLE1) and 97 controls for rare variants in 76 genes.
Prioritization of BLK due to high variant frequency and GWAS support.
Functional validation in human B cell lines and mouse model.
Evidence of epistasis between BLK and BANK1 variants in families.
methods: Variant filtering by MAF <0.005 (rare) and <0.02 (low frequency).
Association tests comparing variant frequencies in patients vs controls.
Luciferase reporter assays for IRF5 and type-I IFN activity.
Co-immunoprecipitation and immunofluorescence for BANK1-TRAF6 interaction.
Generation of Blk R125W knock-in mouse on Faslpr/lpr background.
metrics: Variant frequencies: 10.1% SLE1, 10.9% SLE2 had rare BLK missense variants.
Functional readouts: IRF5 reporter activity, IFNβ luciferase, protein localization.
failures_or_limits: No difference in overall burden of rare variants between patients and controls.
Some variants found in controls were not functionally tested.
Functional assays limited to few variants; not all patient variants characterized.
Mouse model did not develop overt autoimmunity, suggesting incomplete penetrance or redundancy.

section_gists.heading: Discussion
section_gists.gist: The study supports that rare damaging variants in GWAS genes contribute to SLE, with quality of variant (functional impact) more important than quantity. BLK and BANK1 variants converge on increased type-I IFN. Limitations include incomplete penetrance, possible redundancy in mice, and need for additional environmental or genetic factors. Co-segregation of variants in families suggests oligogenic inheritance.
process_signals: Interpretation: rare variants in BLK and BANK1 are damaging and contribute to SLE.
Acknowledgment that not all rare variants are pathogenic; controls had variants but less damaging.
Hypothesis: BLK regulates type-I IFN via BANK1 and sequestosome localization.
Limitations: mouse model did not fully recapitulate human disease; environmental triggers likely needed.
failures_or_limits: Incomplete penetrance in humans and mice.
Redundancy of Src kinases in mice may mask Blk deficiency.
Study focused on two genes; other rare variants not functionally tested.
Sample size limited for rare variant association; replication needed.

section_gists.heading: Methods
section_gists.gist: Two SLE cohorts (n=69 and n=64) and 97 healthy controls were sequenced (WES/WGS). Variants were filtered by MAF and predicted impact. Ethnicity was determined by genetic clustering. Functional studies included luciferase assays, co-IP, immunofluorescence, and mouse model. Batch correction was applied to expression data.
process_signals: Cohorts recruited sequentially; replication built in.
Use of public control datasets (45 and Up, ASPREE).
Stringent variant filtering and confirmation by Sanger sequencing.
Functional assays designed to test specific hypotheses about BLK and BANK1.
methods: WES with SureSelect XT2 All Exon V4; WGS for some samples.
Alignment to Hg19, variant calling with GATK.
Ethnicity determination using GEMTools and 1000Genomes clustering.
PVCA and batch correction for expression data.
failures_or_limits: WES had 21% low/uncovered exon bases vs 4% for WGS, potentially missing variants.
Batch effects in expression data required correction.

global_hypotheses_about_storyline: The study evolved from a broad survey of rare variants in SLE genes to focused functional validation of BLK and BANK1, driven by variant frequency and prior GWAS evidence.
The narrative emphasizes that rare variants are common but only a subset are damaging; functional assays distinguish patient-specific pathogenic variants from benign ones.
The mouse model was used to confirm in vivo relevance but revealed limitations (no overt disease), prompting discussion of additional factors.
uncertainties_to_resolve: Exact p-values and effect sizes for association tests are not fully detailed in provided text.
Functional data for all identified BLK/BANK1 variants not shown; some variants may have been excluded due to lack of effect.
The contribution of other rare variants in the 76 genes remains unexplored.
Population specificity: cohorts predominantly European; generalizability to other ethnicities unclear.
Mechanism linking BLK/BANK1 to type-I IFN via sequestosomes needs further biochemical detail.

events.summary: The study addresses the missing heritability in SLE, hypothesizing that rare coding variants in lupus-risk genes may have strong effects.
The study design includes screening for rare variants in 76 SLE-risk genes in two SLE cohorts and healthy controls.
Rare coding variants in SLE-risk genes were found in 82% of SLE patients and 72% of controls, with no difference in variant type distribution.
BLK was prioritized for functional studies due to high frequency of rare variants and prior GWAS associations.
Rare BLK missense variants were found in ~10% of SLE patients, significantly less in a CVID/CID cohort but comparable to healthy controls.
The study hypothesizes epistasis between BLK and BANK1 variants, noting co-segregation in families with autoimmune disease.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk covers introduction and early results, establishing problem, design, initial screening, and prioritization of BLK and BANK1.

events.summary: Searched for BANK1 W40C in a third cohort of 150 SLE patients using targeted PCR-amplifluor assay.
Postulated that identified BLK SNVs would alter kinase activity by impairing protein availability or function.
Tested whether BLK variants had impaired ability to phosphorylate BANK1; four rare BLK variants showed impaired phosphorylation.
Hypothesized that BLK may share IRF5 as a substrate with LYN and hypomorphic BLK variants may impair IRF5 regulation.
Co-expressed BLK and IRF5 in HEK293T cells; wild-type BLK phosphorylated IRF5, rare BLK variants had diminished or no ability.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk contains hypothesis generation, targeted genotyping, functional assays, and analysis of variant effects on phosphorylation and IFN repression.

events.summary: Tested whether rare BLK variants in SLE patients were more damaging than those in healthy controls by generating plasmids for six control variants and comparing IFNβ repression.
numbers_or_metrics: six rare BLK variants exclusively found in healthy controls
greater than 50% reduction in IFNβ repression

events.summary: Compared repressive ability of all BLK variants side-by-side and found patient variants impaired while control variants did not.
Confirmed BANK1 forms a complex with TRAF6 by coimmunoprecipitation.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk contains key functional experiments comparing patient vs control BLK variants, in vivo mouse model validation, and BANK1 mechanistic studies.

events.summary: IFNβ luciferase assay in HEK293T cells co-transfected with IRF5, MyD88, and BLK variants shows patient variants fail to repress type I IFN.
CRISPR-Cas9 edited Ramos cells homozygous for R131W show impaired repression of IFNβ after R848 stimulation.
Co-immunoprecipitation confirms two-way interaction between BANK1 and TRAF6.

chunk_events.notes: Chunk contains key functional experiments (luciferase assays, CRISPR, mouse model, immunofluorescence, co-IP) and discussion of limitations, directly supporting the paper's narrative on rare variant pathogenicity in BLK and BANK1.

events.summary: Immunofluorescence staining of HEK293T cells expressing wild-type or W40C BANK1-V5 to assess inclusion body formation.
Co-expression of IRF5, TRAF6, and BANK1 variants to measure nuclear IRF5 localization.
CRISPR-Cas9 genome editing of human B-cell lines to introduce specific mutations.
Generation of Blk R125W knock-in mouse via CRISPR-Cas9 zygote microinjection.
Co-immunoprecipitation and western blotting to assess protein interactions and phosphorylation.

chunk_events.notes: This chunk contains detailed methods for functional experiments (immunofluorescence, CRISPR editing, mouse model, qPCR, luciferase assays, co-IP) and data analysis (Limma, QuSAGE). It provides experimental evidence for the functional impact of BANK1 W40C and BLK variants, supporting the paper's core findings.
This chunk contains only references, acknowledgements, author contributions, and license information. No research evolution events are present.

evidence_bank.quote: Another hypothesis is that in some cases SLE arises from rare genetic variants with strong effects.
we selected an initial 69 SLE probands (SLE1), a replication cohort of 64 SLE probands (SLE2) and 97 healthy elderly individuals
82% of SLE patients carried rare nonsynonymous variants in SLE-associated genes as did 72% of healthy controls.
We started by looking at the SNV in BLK given the high frequency of rare variants in this gene.
10.1% of our original SLE patients (SLE1, n = 7 of 69) (Fig. 2a) and 10.9% of our replication cohort (SLE2, n = 7 of 64) had a rare or novel missense SNV in BLK.
we considered the possibility of additional SLE rare variants acting in epistasis with BLK being present in subjects with disease.

when we searched for this variant in a third cohort comprised of 150 SLE patients (for which the DNA yield and quality was insufficient for WES, but adequate for a targeted polymerase chain reaction (PCR)-amplifluor assay)
we postulated that the identified SNVs would alter kinase activity of BLK by impairing availability of protein or protein function.
the four rare BLK variants tested had impaired phosphorylation of BANK1
We thus hypothesized that BLK may share IRF5 as a substrate with LYN and the hypomorphic BLK variants may impair IRF5 regulation.
upon co-expression of BLK and IRF5 in HEK293T cells, wild-type BLK phosphorylated IRF5, whereas the identified rare BLK variants had diminished or no ability to phosphorylate IRF5
we generated plasmids expressing each of the six rare BLK variants exclusively found in healthy controls
tested side-by-side the repressive ability of all BLK variants
none of the six rare BLK variants found exclusively in healthy controls had a similar impairment (p<0.0001)

event_graph.paper_main_problem: SLE is a heterogeneous autoimmune disease with a strong genetic component, but common GWAS variants explain only a modest fraction of risk. The study investigates whether rare coding variants in known SLE-risk genes contribute to disease, focusing on functional consequences in BLK and BANK1.

canonical_events.summary: The study addresses the missing heritability in SLE, hypothesizing that rare coding variants in lupus-risk genes may have strong effects.
The study design includes screening for rare variants in 76 SLE-risk genes in two SLE cohorts and healthy controls using WES/WGS.
Rare coding variants in SLE-risk genes were found in 82% of SLE patients and 72% of controls, with no difference in variant type distribution.
BLK was prioritized for functional studies due to high frequency of rare variants and prior GWAS associations.
Rare BLK missense variants were found in ~10% of SLE patients, significantly less in a CVID/CID cohort but comparable to healthy controls.
The study hypothesizes epistasis between BLK and BANK1 variants, noting co-segregation in families with autoimmune disease.
Searched for BANK1 W40C in a third cohort of 150 SLE patients using targeted PCR-amplifluor assay.
Postulated that identified BLK SNVs would alter kinase activity by impairing protein availability or function.
Tested whether BLK variants had impaired ability to phosphorylate BANK1; four rare BLK variants showed impaired phosphorylation.
Hypothesized that BLK may share IRF5 as a substrate with LYN and hypomorphic BLK variants may impair IRF5 regulation.
Co-expressed BLK and IRF5 in HEK293T cells; wild-type BLK phosphorylated IRF5, rare BLK variants had diminished or no ability.
Tested whether rare BLK variants in SLE patients were more damaging than those in healthy controls by generating plasmids for six control variants and comparing IFNβ repression.
key_numbers: six rare BLK variants exclusively found in healthy controls
greater than 50% reduction in IFNβ repression

canonical_events.summary: Compared repressive ability of all BLK variants side-by-side and found patient variants impaired while control variants did not (p<0.0001).
IFNβ luciferase assay in HEK293T cells co-transfected with IRF5, MyD88, and BLK variants shows patient variants fail to repress type I IFN.
CRISPR-Cas9 edited Ramos cells homozygous for R131W show impaired repression of IFNβ after R848 stimulation.
Confirmed BANK1 forms a complex with TRAF6 by coimmunoprecipitation.
Immunofluorescence staining of HEK293T cells expressing wild-type or W40C BANK1-V5 to assess inclusion body formation.
Co-expression of IRF5, TRAF6, and BANK1 variants to measure nuclear IRF5 localization.

edges.reason: The problem of missing heritability motivates the design of screening for rare variants.
Screening yields initial variant frequency results.
High variant frequency in BLK leads to prioritization for functional studies.
Detailed analysis of BLK variant frequencies in cohorts.
Observation of co-segregation leads to epistasis hypothesis.
Epistasis hypothesis prompts targeted search for BANK1 W40C.
Identification of BLK variants leads to hypothesis about kinase activity impairment.
Impaired BANK1 phosphorylation suggests IRF5 as shared substrate.
Hypothesis tested by co-expression and phosphorylation assay.
Functional impairment leads to comparison of patient vs control variants.
Repression impairment confirmed in IFNβ luciferase assay.
CRISPR-edited B-cell line confirms impaired IFNβ repression.
Identification of BANK1 W40C leads to validation of TRAF6 interaction.
TRAF6 interaction prompts investigation of inclusion body formation.
Altered inclusion bodies lead to measurement of nuclear IRF5 localization.

must_cover_points: Rare variant screening in SLE-risk genes
Functional assays showing impaired phosphorylation and IFNβ repression
